title: Forkify Capstone Retrospective

learningObjectives.text: Explain architecture review using a new example and justify the result.
Choose and justify trade-offs using a new example and justify the result.
Explain what to carry into future projects using a new example and justify the result.
learningObjectives.subtopic: What to carry into future projects

whyThisMatters: This topic turns forkify capstone retrospective from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Application Architecture: Forkify** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Architecture review** → **Trade-offs** → **What to carry into future projects**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Architecture review
sections.body: A retrospective compares outcomes with goals, identifies successful decisions and accidental complexity, and records lessons that should influence the next project.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.body: Evaluate alternatives by complexity, clarity, testability, performance, team understanding, and likely change rather than searching for a universally perfect pattern.

sections.title: What to carry into future projects
sections.body: A retrospective compares outcomes with goals, identifies successful decisions and accidental complexity, and records lessons that should influence the next project.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Forkify Capstone Retrospective connects Architecture review, Trade-offs, What to carry into future projects.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

questions.prompt: A team is making a decision involving Forkify Capstone Retrospective. Which approach shows the strongest understanding of **Architecture review**?

choices.label: State the requirement and assumptions, apply the Architecture review model, and verify the observable consequence before adding complexity.
choices.feedback: Transferable understanding of Architecture review means using the model to make and verify a decision in a new context.

choices.label: Use the term Architecture review in documentation without changing any decision or behavior.

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes **Architecture review** from **Trade-offs** in this topic?

choices.label: They address different parts of the topic's model; a correct decision identifies which one explains the current requirement and how the two interact.
choices.feedback: The concepts are related but should be applied according to the requirement and the role each plays in Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: They are interchangeable labels, so choosing between them has no effect on reasoning.

questions.prompt: A developer notices this recurring mistake: “Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.” What is the best response?

choices.label: Return to the requirement, use the Forkify Capstone Retrospective model to identify the failed assumption, and verify the corrected behavior with a representative case.
choices.feedback: The useful response corrects both the misconception and the next development action for Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: Hide the symptom and continue so the schedule is not affected.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Forkify Capstone Retrospective rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Add unrelated abstractions before reproducing the problem.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Forkify Capstone Retrospective rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Treat the mistake as unavoidable and remove the relevant check.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Forkify Capstone Retrospective rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: The useful response corrects both the misconception and the next development action for Forkify Capstone Retrospective.
questions.hint: Choose the response that makes the failure reproducible and the correction verifiable.

questions.prompt: Which day-to-day workflow is most reliable when a task depends on Forkify Capstone Retrospective?

choices.label: Clarify the requirement, identify the relevant model or boundary, test the smallest representative case, then refactor after the behavior is understood.
choices.feedback: A reliable workflow makes assumptions and failures visible early, then improves the implementation after correctness is established.

choices.label: Start with the largest available abstraction and postpone defining expected behavior.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Forkify Capstone Retrospective rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Copy a solution from another project without checking whether its assumptions match.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Forkify Capstone Retrospective rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Optimize for line count first and inspect correctness only after release.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Forkify Capstone Retrospective rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: A reliable workflow makes assumptions and failures visible early, then improves the implementation after correctness is established.
questions.hint: Prefer the sequence that produces evidence early and keeps decisions reversible.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best engineering judgment about Forkify Capstone Retrospective?

choices.label: Use Forkify Capstone Retrospective to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.
choices.feedback: Use Forkify Capstone Retrospective to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.

choices.label: Use Forkify Capstone Retrospective in every file because more abstraction always improves software.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Forkify Capstone Retrospective rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Avoid Forkify Capstone Retrospective completely because every additional concept reduces maintainability.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Forkify Capstone Retrospective rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Choose only by the number of lines and ignore correctness, ownership, and future change.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Forkify Capstone Retrospective rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: Use Forkify Capstone Retrospective to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.
questions.hint: Choose the option that balances correctness, clarity, cost, and the actual requirement.

researchSources.title: Wrapping Up: Final Considerations
researchSources.usage: Coverage research only; no transcript wording is canonical.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.

assessmentProfile.label: Developer workflow and decision scenarios
assessmentProfile.rationale: The learner should make realistic development decisions using requirements, logs, file structures, configuration, or short code artifacts.
assessmentProfile.inLessonPolicy: Use concise concept checks; coding and hybrid topics include at least one code-reading check.
assessmentProfile.topicQuizPolicy: Questions Q04–Q08 form the topic quiz. The presentation mix follows this profile.
